description: Action research / case study pattern used by students at Colleges of Education affiliated with UEW and UCC. Focus on classroom-level educational research.

chapter1.title: INTRODUCTION
chapter1.writingPattern: Opens with policy context (GES/NaCCA/curriculum). Problem statement grounded in classroom observation or school data. Objectives use action verbs.
chapter1.tense: Present for context, past for specific observations

chapter2.title: REVIEW OF RELATED LITERATURE
chapter2.writingPattern: Thematic organisation. Heavy use of GES policy documents, NaCCA curriculum documents, and MOE publications alongside academic journals. References Common Core Programme or B7 curriculum where relevant.
chapter2.voice: Third person. Synthesises, not just describes.

chapter3.title: RESEARCH METHODOLOGY
chapter3.writingPattern: Specifies school, district, and exact context. Action research or case study design common. References Creswell (2018) or other standard methodology texts.

chapter4.title: DATA PRESENTATION AND DISCUSSION
chapter4.writingPattern: Each research question answered separately with data. Tables and figures used heavily. Findings linked to literature review. Discusses implications for classroom practice.
chapter4.tense: Past for data, present for interpretation

chapter5.title: SUMMARY, CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS
chapter5.writingPattern: Recommendations directed at multiple stakeholders: GES, school heads, classroom teachers, policymakers. Each recommendation supported by a finding.
chapter5.tense: Past for summary, present for conclusions

examples.chapter1Opening: The introduction of the Common Core Programme (CCP) by the National Council for Curriculum and Assessment (NaCCA) in 2019 represented a paradigm shift in Ghana's basic education curriculum. The CCP, which replaced the old curriculum for Junior High School students, emphasises critical thinking, problem-solving, and creativity as core competencies (NaCCA, 2019). Despite the policy's ambitious goals, early classroom observations suggest that many teachers in the Ashanti Region struggle to implement the competency-based approach effectively (Acheampong, 2022).
examples.chapter2Synthesis: Teacher preparedness has been identified as a critical factor in curriculum implementation. Adu-Gyamfi and Akyempong (2020) surveyed 150 teachers in the Greater Accra Region and found that only 34.7% felt adequately prepared to deliver the Common Core Programme. This finding aligns with Owusu-Mensah (2021) who observed that teachers in rural schools received minimal in-service training on the new curriculum. However, Asare and Antwi (2022) reported that teachers who participated in NaCCA's district-level workshops demonstrated significantly higher self-efficacy in curriculum delivery.
examples.chapter3Context: The study was conducted at St. John's Lutheran School, a public basic school in the Kumasi Metropolitan Area of the Ashanti Region. The school has a population of 245 pupils in Junior High School forms 1-3 and 12 teachers. The school was selected because it represents a typical urban public school in the region, with similar resource constraints and teacher profiles as many other schools in the district.
examples.chapter4Finding: The data presented in Table 4.1 shows that 58.3% of teachers had received less than 5 hours of training on the Common Core Programme. This finding is consistent with Adu-Gyamfi and Akyempong (2020) who reported low training hours among teachers in the Greater Accra Region. The implication is that many teachers are implementing the curriculum without adequate understanding of its competency-based approach, which may explain the low pupil engagement observed during classroom visits.
